elements.sent-2411-001: This paper proposes a new autoregressive model as an effective and scalable monocular depth estimator.
elements.sent-2411-002: Our idea is simple: We tackle the monocular depth estimation (MDE) task with an autoregressive prediction paradigm, based on two core designs.
elements.sent-2411-003: First, our depth autoregressive model (DAR) treats the depth map of different resolutions as a set of tokens, and conducts the low-to-high resolution autoregressive objective with a patch-wise casual mask.
elements.sent-2411-004: Second, our DAR recursively discretizes the entire depth range into more compact intervals, and attains the coarse-to-fine granularity autoregressive objective in an ordinal-regression manner.
elements.sent-2411-005: By coupling these two autoregressive objectives, our DAR establishes new state-of-the-art (SOTA) on KITTI and NYU Depth v2 by clear margins.
elements.sent-2411-006: Further, our scalable approach allows us to scale the model up to 2.0B and achieve the best RMSE of 1.799 on the KITTI dataset (5\% improvement) compared to 1.896 by the current SOTA (Depth Anything).
elements.sent-2411-007: DAR further showcases zero-shot generalization ability on unseen datasets.
elements.sent-2411-008: These results suggest that DAR yields superior performance with an autoregressive prediction paradigm, providing a promising approach to equip modern autoregressive large models (e.g., GPT-4o) with depth estimation capabilities.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.43\textwidth]{figs/111.pdf} \caption{We exploit two ``order" properties of the MDE task that can be transformed into two autoregressive objectives. {\bf (a) Resolution autoregressive objective:} The generation of depth maps can follow a resolution order from low to high. For each step of the resolution autoregressive process, the Transformer predicts the next higher-resolution token map conditioned on all the previous ones. {\bf (b) Granularity autoregressive objective:} The range of depth values is ordered, from 0 to specific max values. For each step of the granularity autoregressive process, we increase exponentially the number of bins (e.g., doubling the bin number), and utilize the previous predictions to predict a more refined depth with a smaller and more refined granularity. Our proposed DAR aims to perform these two autoregressive processes simultaneously.} \label{fig1} em
elements.sent-2411-009: The Monocular Depth Estimation (MDE) task aims to predict per pixel depth from a single RGB image, which plays a crucial role in scene understanding and reconstruction~\cite{izadi2011kinectfusion,chen2019towards .}
elements.sent-2411-010: This task has broad applications, including autonomous driving~\cite{wang2019pseudo , robotics~\cite{jia2023object , augmented reality~\cite{oney2020evaluation , medical endoscopic surgery~\cite{liu2019dense , etc. Most deep learning (DL) based methods typically followed top-down-bottom-up encoder-decoder architectures~\cite{eigen2014depth,laina2016deeper,yuan2022neural,patil2022p3depth,liu2023va,aich2021bidirectional,yang2021transformer,agarwal2023attention,li2023depthformer,duan2023diffusiondepth,ji2023ddp,zhao2023unleashing,saxena2023monocular,ke2024repurposing , which extract and fuse low-level and high-level features for depth estimation.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: Recently, autoregressive (AR) architectures have demonstrated strong generalization capabilities and significant scalability across a multitude of tasks: (i) Generalization: AR models achieve remarkable zero- and few-shot performance on previously unseen datasets, and exhibit substantial flexibility in adapting to diverse downstream tasks~\cite{radford2019language,brown2020language .}
elements.sent-2411-012: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs)~\cite{radford2018improving,radford2019language,brown2020language and multi-modal large language models (MLLMs) (e.g., GPT-4 and LLaVA~\cite{achiam2023gpt,liu2024visual ), built upon AR architectures, achieved outstanding results in a wide range of tasks, including text-to-image generation~\cite{esser2021taming,lee2022autoregressive,tian2024visual , video generation~\cite{yan2021videogpt,wu2022nuwa , object detection~\cite{chen2021pix2seq and tracking~\cite{wei2023autoregressive , among others.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: This naturally leads to an interesting question: Can an autoregressive model be developed for the MDE task? However, autoregressive modeling relies on a well-organized sequential data formation, where each step’s prediction is logically connected to the previous steps.
elements.sent-2411-014: While this sequential dependency may be common in other tasks, it is not intuitively align with the MDE requirements, where meaningful sequential prediction targets are less apparent.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.47\textwidth]{figs/p111.pdf} \caption{RMSE performances ($\downarrow$) {\textit vs.} model sizes on the KITTI dataset. Our DAR shows strong scalability and achieves better performance-efficiency trade-off among cutting-edge methods.} \label{figp} em
elements.sent-2411-015: In this paper, we introduce a simple, effective, and scalable depth autoregressive (DAR) framework for MDE.
elements.sent-2411-016: Our new approach leverages two key ordering properties of MDE, and we incorporate them as autoregressive objectives, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig1 .}
elements.sent-2411-017: The first property is ``depth map resolution'': We generate depth maps at varying resolutions, ordered from low to high, and treat the sequences of depth maps of different resolutions as prediction targets~\cite{tian2024visual .}
elements.sent-2411-018: This approach reframes depth map generation as a low-to-high resolution autoregressive objective, where each step generates a higher-resolution depth map based on previous predictions.
elements.sent-2411-019: The second property is ``depth values", which inherently exist in a continuous space.
elements.sent-2411-020: Typically, known methods discretize the depth values into several intervals (or bins) to formulate an ordinal regression task~\cite{fu2018deep .}
elements.sent-2411-021: By further discretizing the depth range into progressively finer intervals, we recast MDE as a coarse-to-fine autoregressive objective.
elements.sent-2411-022: For the resolution autoregressive objective, we develop a depth autoregressive Transformer that predicts the next-resolution depth map based on its prefix predictions with the patch-wise causal mask.
elements.sent-2411-023: To achieve the granularity autoregressive objective, we propose a novel binning strategy called Multiway Tree Bins (MTBin), which queries the corresponding bin using prior depth predictions and then recursively refines each bin into sub-bins with error tolerance for subsequent autoregressive steps.
elements.sent-2411-024: Importantly, these bins are used to not only compute the final depth values but also embed granularity information into the latent token maps, effectively guiding the depth map generation process.
elements.sent-2411-025: Comprehensive experiments show that our DAR achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance on the KITTI and NYU Depth v2 datasets.
elements.sent-2411-026: With a similar model size, DAR outperforms current SOTA (Depth Anything) in all the metrics, particularly by 3\% in the RMSE metric on KITTI.
elements.sent-2411-027: DAR also exhibits scaling laws akin to those witnessed in LLMs, and its size can be easily scaled up to 2.0B, which establishes a new SOTA performance, as shown in Fig.~\ref{figp .}
elements.sent-2411-028: Lastly, we showcase DAR’s zero-shot generalization capabilities on unseen datasets.
elements.sent-2411-029: These results further validate the generalizability and scalability of DAR.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=0.85\textwidth]{figs/overview12.pdf} \caption{{\bf An overview of DAR.} We begin with encoding the input RGB images into image tokens as the context condition. At each step, DAR Transformer with the patch-wise causal mask performs autoregressive predictions, that is, it allows the input token map (upsampled from the previous resolution token map $r_{k-1}$) to interact with only the prefix tokens and global image feature tokens for the next-resolution token map modeling. The output latent tokens are then sent to the ConvGRU module, which injects the prompts of new refined bin candidates $\mathbf{c}_{k}$ (generated by MTBin from the previous prediction $\tilde{D}_{k-1}$) for further granularity guidance and generates the next-resolution token map $r_{k}$. The new depth map $\tilde{D}_{k}$ is generated by a linear combination of the next-granularity bin candidates $\mathbf{c}_{k}$ and softmax value $\mathbf{p}_{k}$ of the next-resolution token map, achieving concurrently a resolution and granularity autoregressive evolution. } \label{fig2} em
elements.sent-2411-030: The contributions of this work are multi-fold:.
elements.sent-2411-031: To the best of our knowledge, we introduce the first autoregressive model for monocular depth estimation (MDE), called DAR — a simple, effective, and scalable framework.
elements.sent-2411-032: Our key insight lies in transforming two ordered properties in MDE, depth map resolution and granularity, into autoregressive objectives.
elements.sent-2411-033: We reformulate the low- and high-level feature fusion process in existing encoder-decoder models as a low-to-high resolution autoregressive objective.
elements.sent-2411-034: We introduce a new Depth autoregressive Transformer that uses the patch-wise causal mask and progressively generates depth maps at increasing resolutions, conditioned on tokens from the input RGB images.
elements.sent-2411-035: We propose a novel binning strategy called multiway tree bins (MTBin), tailored for the granularity autoregressive objective, which transforms MDE tasks into an autoregressive bin sequence prediction task.
elements.sent-2411-036: By embedding bin information into the latent token map, DAR effectively connects the resolution and granularity autoregressive processes.
elements.sent-2411-037: DAR establishes new state-of-the-art performance on the KITTI and NYU Depth v2 datasets, and exhibits stronger zero-shot capability than Depth Anything~\cite{yang2024depth .}
elements.sent-2411-038: A series of DAR models, ranging from 440M to 2.0B parameters, is developed based on decoder-only Transformer~\cite{vaswani2017attention,radford2019language .}
elements.sent-2411-039: The largest model achieves 0.205 RMSE and 0.982 $\delta_{1}$ on NYU Depth v2, largely outperforming the existing methods.
elements.sent-2411-040: Monocular Depth Estimation has a long development of methods, ranging from traditional methods to deep learning (DL) techniques.
elements.sent-2411-041: Traditional methods relied on handcrafted features~\cite{hoiem2007recovering,liu2008sift and used Markov Random Fields~\cite{saxena2007learning to predict depth maps.}}
elements.sent-2411-042: However, they suffered limitations in handling complex scenes.
elements.sent-2411-043: Modern DL techniques approached the problem as a dense regression problem.
elements.sent-2411-044: Successive improvements mainly came from three fronts: model architecture, data-driven, and language guidance.
elements.sent-2411-045: ZoeDepth~\cite{bhat2023zoedepth and Depth Anything~\cite{yang2024depth further leveraged the benefits of large-scale unlabeled data (62M) via self-supervised learning to obtain outstanding zero-shot generalization.}}
elements.sent-2411-046: Another main line of work is to treat MDE as an ordinal regression task~\cite{herbrich1999support,harrell2012regression , which aims to predict labels on an ordinal scale.}
elements.sent-2411-047: By discretizing the depth space into several bins, DORN~\cite{fu2018deep first proposed an ordinal regression network to predict discrete depth values.}
elements.sent-2411-048: Since then, many studies~\cite{bhat2021adabins,shao2024iebins,li2024binsformer developed various depth binning strategies to discretize depth range and solve MDE as ordinal regression.}
elements.sent-2411-049: In particular, Ord2Seq~\cite{wang2023ord2seq treated ordinal regression as a label sequence task, and proposed an autoregressive network to predict the more refined labels progressively.}
elements.sent-2411-050: Inspired by this, we transform MDE as an autoregressive prediction task from a bin perspective to achieve more granular prediction progressively.
elements.sent-2411-051: Many recent methods~\cite{esser2021taming,razavi2019generating,lee2022autoregressive explored the effectiveness of autoregressive models in the visual domain.}
elements.sent-2411-052: VQGAN~\cite{esser2021taming proposed to use VQVAE~\cite{van2017neural to conduct the autoregressive process in the latent space.}}
elements.sent-2411-053: It employed GPT-2 decoder-only Transformer to generate tokens in the raster-scan order.
elements.sent-2411-054: VQVAE-2~\cite{razavi2019generating and RQ-Transformer~\cite{lee2022autoregressive also followed this raster-scan manner, but used extra scales or stacked codes.}}
elements.sent-2411-055: VAR~\cite{tian2024visual proposed text-to-image autoregressive generation via next-scale prediction, which transforms the entire image into a set of tokens and treats them as input to predict the next-scale target image.}
elements.sent-2411-056: Inspired by this, we transform MDE as an autoregressive prediction task from a scale perspective to achieve larger resolution predictions progressively.
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=0.45\textwidth]{figs/44.pdf} \caption{Illustrating the patch-wise casual mask for ensuring that the current token map can interact only with tokens from itself and the prefix token maps.} \label{fig4} em
elements.sent-2411-057: Some methods~\cite{fu2018deep,bhat2021adabins tackled MDE in an ordinal regression fashion, learning the probabilistic distribution on each pixel and using a linear combination with depth candidates as the final depth prediction.}
elements.sent-2411-058: Suppose we divide the depth range $[L, R]$ into $N$ bins.
elements.sent-2411-060: For each pixel, the model will predict $N$ Softmax scores $\{\mathbf{p _{1 , \mathbf{p _2,\ldots, \mathbf{p _N\}$, referring to the probabilities over the $N$ bins.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-061: The final predicted depth value $\tilde{D}$ is calculated from the linear combination of Softmax scores and bin centers at this pixel, as: \begin{equation}\tilde{D}=\sum_{i=1}^N\mathbf{c}_i\mathbf{p}_i.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-062: We propose a new Depth AutoRegressive (DAR) modeling approach to explore the potential of autoregressive models in dealing with the depth estimation task.
elements.sent-2411-063: We define the task as follows: Given an input RGB image $\mathcal{I \in \mathbb{R ^{3\times H \times W}$, where $H$ and $W$ are the height and width of $\mathcal{I}$ respectively, predict the depth map $\tilde{\mathcal{D }$ of $\mathcal{I}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-064: Our model predicts the depth maps of different scales $\{\mathcal{\tilde{D _{1 , \mathcal{\tilde{D _{2 , \ldots,\mathcal{\tilde{D _{K}\}$ progressively in an autoregressive process.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-065: That is, each depth map at step $k$ is conditioned by the previous predictions, as: { \begin{equation}p(\mathcal{\tilde{D}}_1,\mathcal{\tilde{D}}_2,\ldots,\mathcal{\tilde{D}}_K)=\!\prod_{k=1}^Kp_{\theta}(\mathcal{\tilde{D}}_k\mid \mathcal{\tilde{D}}_1,\mathcal{\tilde{D}}_2,\ldots,\mathcal{\tilde{D}}_{k-1}).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-066: Fig.~\ref{fig2 shows an overview of our proposed DAR.}
elements.sent-2411-067: DAR involves two autoregressive objectives with ordinal properties: resolution and granularity.
elements.sent-2411-068: The former, resolution autoregressive objective, aims to predict the depth map from low resolution to high resolution; the latter, granularity autoregressive objective, aims to predict the depth map from coarse granularity to fine granularity.
elements.sent-2411-069: Specifically, DAR consists of four parts: em.
elements.sent-2411-070: Image Encoder: We apply an Image Encoder to extract RGB image features, which extracts image features into imaging tokens with latent representations.
elements.sent-2411-071: DAR Transformer: Our DAR Transformer can progressively predict different resolution token maps conditioned on extracted RGB imaging tokens via the patch-wise casual mask.
elements.sent-2411-072: Multiway Tree Bins (MTBin): We develop a Multiway Tree Bins strategy to transform the depth range of each pixel into different granularity (number) bins.
elements.sent-2411-073: Bins Injection: Bins Injection utilizes the bin candidates' information to guide the refinement of the latent features of the depth token maps.
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=0.467\textwidth]{figs/55.pdf} \caption{A schematic diagram of the multiway tree bins strategy.} \label{fig3} em
elements.sent-2411-074: This section elaborates on our DAR Transformer with the patch-wise causal attention mask for the resolution autoregressive objective.. {\bf DAR Transformer.}
elements.sent-2411-075: Our DAR Transformer follows the vanilla architecture in~\cite{vaswani2017attention , composed of Multi-headed Self-Attention (MSA), Layer Normalization (LN), and Multi-headed Cross-Attention (MCA) layers with residual connections, aiming to predict the logits sequence of different scale.}
elements.sent-2411-076: Unlike text-to-image models that use class labels as the condition, depth estimation is mainly based on input RGB image features.
elements.sent-2411-077: Thus, we take the image features $X$ as the condition to control depth estimation.
elements.sent-2411-078: At each step $k$, we first upsample the token map $r_{k-1}$ of the previous step to the next resolution as the input token map $y_{in ^{k}$.}
elements.sent-2411-079: The DAR Transformer takes $y_{in ^{k}$ as input query and sends it to the MSA and MCA layers to finally produce the logits $y_{out ^{k}$, where MSA takes the previous token maps $y_{in ^{1:k}$ to compute keys and values via the patch-wise causal attention mask, and MCA takes image features $X$ for attention calculation.}}}
elements.sent-2411-080: We formulate the process at time step $k$ as: \begin{equation} \begin{aligned} y_{k} = \text{Upsamping}(r_{k-1}), y_{hidden}^{k} =\text{LN}(\text{MSA}(y_{k}W_{Q};y_{1:k}W_{K};y_{1:k}W_{V}; Mask)), y_{out}^{k} = \text{LN}(\text{MCA}(y_{hidden}^{k};X)), \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $W_{Q}$, $W_{K}$, and $W_{V}$ are weight matrices for computing queries, keys, and values, and Mask denotes the patch-wise causal attention mask tailored for the next-resolution autoregressive paradigm (to be discussed below).
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation} y_{k} = Upsamping(r_{k-1}), y_{hidden}^{k} =LN(MSA(y_{k}W_{Q};y_{1:k}W_{K};y_{1:k}W_{V}; Mask)), y_{out}^{k} = LN(MCA(y_{hidden}^{k};X)), \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-081: The logits $y_{out ^k$ are then used to generate a latent token map $r_{k}$ of step $k$ guided by the Bins Injection module (to be discussed below).}
elements.sent-2411-082: During this process, DAR can integrate previous knowledge, global image features, and bin candidate information to accommodate the detailed feature requirements for generating a higher-resolution depth map.
elements.sent-2411-083: Note that to achieve ``next-resolution'' prediction, we adopt a patch-wise causal attention mask that treats the entire token map as a merged patch-wise token, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig4 .}
elements.sent-2411-084: This novel mask can ensure that each token of $y_{k}$ can interact only with its prefix tokens belonging to $y_{\leq k}$ and other tokens within $y_{k}$.
elements.sent-2411-085: To achieve the autoregressive objective on granularity, we introduce two core modules: Multiway Tree Bins (MTBin) and Bins Injection.
elements.figure-2411-006: \caption{Model sizes and architecture configurations of DAR. } 0.9{ \begin{tabular}{lcccc} Model Model Size Layers Hidden Size Heads DAR-Small 440M 5 1024 4 DAR-Base 1B 7 1536 8 DAR-Large 2B 13 2560 12 \end{tabular} } \label{par-tab} em
elements.sent-2411-086: This strategy aims to use the previous depth map's prediction to generate smaller bins for the next step's finer-grained depth predictions.
elements.sent-2411-087: Instead of using a fixed number of bins with equal-size intervals, MTBin recursively searches for more high-quality depth by progressively reducing the search range, as illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig3 .}
elements.sent-2411-088: Assuming that at step $k-1$, the depth range is divided into $N$ bins in the uniform space, as: \begin{equation}[\mathbf{b}_{k-1}^{1}, \mathbf{b}_{k-1}^{2},\ldots,\mathbf{b}_{k-1}^{N+1}],\end{equation} em where $\mathbf{b _{k-1 ^{i}$ represents the left boundary of the $i$-th bin in step $k-1$, except $\mathbf{b _{k-1 ^{N+1}$ represents the right boundary of the $N$-th bin.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-089: Suppose the predicted depth $\tilde{D _{k-1 (\mathbf{x )$ for a pixel $\mathbf{x}$ lies in the $t$-th target bin $[\mathbf{b _{k-1 ^{t , \mathbf{b _{k-1 ^{t+1 ]$, that is: \begin{equation}\mathbf{b}_{k-1}^{t} \leq \tilde{D}_{k-1}(\mathbf{x}) \leq \mathbf{b}_{k-1}^{t+1}.\end{equation}.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-090: However, the ground truth may fall outside of the target bin, due to depth prediction error.
elements.sent-2411-091: Thus, to maintain the model's error tolerance, the new depth range first will be expanded to adjacent bins $[\mathbf{b ^{t-1 _{k-1 , \mathbf{b ^{t+2 _{k-1 ]$, which is then split into sub-bins in the uniform space.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-092: This process can be formulated as: \begin{equation}L = \frac{b_{k-1}^{\min\{t+2, N+1\}} - b_{k-1}^{\max\{t-1, 1\}}}{N}, b_{k}^{i} = b_{k-1}^{t-1} + (i-1)\cdot L, \ i = 1, 2,\ldots, N,\end{equation} em where $\min\{t+2, N+1\}$ and $\max\{t-1, 1\}$ are for avoiding out-of-right/left boundaries.
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation}L = \frac{b_{k-1}^{\min\{t+2, N+1\}} - b_{k-1}^{\max\{t-1, 1\}}}{N}, b_{k}^{i} = b_{k-1}^{t-1} + (i-1)\cdot L, \ i = 1, 2,\ldots, N,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-093: Since this splitting process looks like a multiway tree, we call this concept Multiway Tree Bins.
elements.sent-2411-094: Each pixel's decision process is unique, ranging from coarse to fine granularity progressively.
elements.sent-2411-095: These sub-bins will serve as new bin candidates to further guide the modeling of depth features via Bins Injection (discussed below) and perform the linear combination with the Softmax values of the token maps predicted by the model to obtain finer-grained depth maps.
elements.sent-2411-096: Specifically, we can take the bin centers as the depth candidates at step $k$, formulated as: \begin{equation}\mathbf{c}_{k}^{i} = \frac{\mathbf{b}_{k}^{i}+\mathbf{b}_{k}^{i+1}}{2}, i = 1, 2,\ldots, N,\end{equation} where $\mathbf{c _{\mathbf{i }$ represents the $i$-th bin center (depth candidate).}}
elements.sent-2411-097: When we obtain the per-pixel Softmax values of $r_{k}$, i.e., the probabilistic distribution $\mathbf{p _{k}$ associated with depth candidates, we compute the final depth via a linear combination: \begin{equation}\tilde{D}_{k}(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N}\mathbf{c}_{k}^{i}\cdot\mathbf{p}_{k}^{i}\left(\mathbf{x}\right),\end{equation} em where $\tilde{D _{k (\mathbf{x )$ and $\mathbf{p _{k ^{i (\mathbf{x )$ represent the predicted depth and $i$-th depth candidate probability of pixel $\mathbf{x}$ at autoregressive step $k$, respectively.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-098: Notably, at first we initialize the full depth range $[d_{min ,d_{max ]$ into $N$ bins in the uniform space: \begin{equation}\mathbf{b}_{1}^{i} = d_{min} + (i - 1)\cdot L, \ i = 1, 2,\ldots, N,\end{equation} em where $\mathbf{b _{1 ^{i}$ represents the left boundary of the $i$-th bin at step 1, $N$ is 16 by default, [$d_{min}$, $d_{max}$] are [0.1, 10] and [0.1, 80] for NYU Depth V2 and KITTI, respectively, and $L$ denotes the bin width that is equal to $\frac{d_{max -d_{min {N}$.}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-099: Bins Injection module aims to utilize the new valid depth range and bin candidates to guide the modeling of depth features.
elements.sent-2411-100: First, we project the depth candidates $\mathbf{c ^{k}$ into the feature space via $3 \times 3$ convolutional layers.}
elements.sent-2411-101: Then, the obtained bin features $\mathbf{f _{bin ^{k}$ as the context, are used to further guide the output features of the DAR Transformer via a ConvGRU~\cite{teed2020raft module.}}}
elements.sent-2411-102: We formulate this process as: \begin{equation}f_{bin}^{k} = Conv_{3 \times 3} (c^{k}), r_{k} = ConvGRU(y_{out}^{k};f_{bin}^{k}).\end{equation}. em.
elements.sent-2411-103: To ensure a fair comparison with existing methods, we choose ViT as the Image Encoder (which is the same as Depth Anything).
elements.sent-2411-104: The conditioned image feature tokens are obtained by aggregating the feature maps from different layers of the Image Encoder, by bringing them all to 1/8 resolution of the input image, resulting in a token map of size $1536 \times H/8 \times W/8$, where $H$ and $W$ are the height and width of the input RGB image.. {\bf Loss Function.}
elements.sent-2411-105: Since the ground-truth depth map has missing values, we cannot resize the ground-truth to different resolutions.
elements.sent-2411-106: Thus, we upsample all the predicted depth maps to the same size of the ground-truth, and calculate and leverage the scaled Scale-Invariant Loss: { \begin{equation}\mathcal{L} =\sum_{k=1}^K\alpha\sqrt{\frac1{|\mathbf{T}|}\sum_{\mathbf{x \in T}}\left(\mathbf{g}_{k}\left(\mathbf{x}\right)\right)^2-\frac\beta{|\mathbf{T}|^2}\left(\sum_{\mathbf{x \in T}}\mathbf{g}_{k}\left(\mathbf{x}\right)\right)^2}\!,\end{equation} where $\mathbf{g _{k (\mathbf{x ) = \text{log \tilde{\mathcal{D _{k (\mathbf{x ) - \text{log}\mathcal{D _{gt (\mathbf{x )$, $K$ is the maximum number of steps and is set to 5, $T$ represents the set of pixels having valid ground-truth values, and $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are set to 10 and 0.85 based on~\cite{lee2019big .}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-107: Following~\cite{kaplan2020scaling,henighan2020scaling,hoffmann2022training,achiam2023gpt , we scale the model up with different sizes of the DAR Transformer.}
elements.sent-2411-108: The configurations are shown in Table~\ref{par-tab .. {\bf Implementation Details.}}
elements.sent-2411-109: Our model is implemented on the PyTorch platform.
elements.sent-2411-110: For optimization, the AdamW optimizer~\cite{kingma2014adam is used with an initial learning rate of $3 \times 10^{-5}$.}
elements.sent-2411-111: We first linearly increase the learning rate to $5 \times 10^{-4}$, and then linearly decrease it across the training iterations.
elements.sent-2411-113: We train our model for 25 epochs for both the KITTI and NYU Depth v2 datasets.
elements.sent-2411-114: In each step, the number of new bins is $N=16$.
elements.sent-2411-115: For the DAR-Base model, each epoch takes $\sim$30 minutes to train using 8 NVIDIA A100 GPUs.. {\bf Datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-116: We conduct experiments on three benchmark datasets including NYU Depth V2~\cite{silberman2012indoor , KITTI~\cite{geiger2012we , and SUN RGB-D~\cite{song2015sun .}}}
elements.sent-2411-117: We follow the train-test split in~\cite{lee2019monocular , which uses 24,231 images for training and 654 images for testing.}
elements.sent-2411-118: The ground truth depth maps were obtained using a structured light sensor with a resolution of $640 \times 480$.
elements.sent-2411-119: The official split provides 42,949 images for training, 1,000 for validation, and 500 for testing, with resolution $352 \times 1216$.
elements.sent-2411-120: The depth values range from 0 to 10 meters.
elements.sent-2411-121: We use only the official test set (5050 images) for zero-shot evaluation.

elements.sent-2411-122: We report the results in Table~\ref{tab1 .}
elements.sent-2411-123: As one can see, DAR with the same pretrained backbone and similar model size performs better than and above all the known methods.
elements.sent-2411-124: By scaling up, DAR-Large establishes a new SOTA performance in all the metrics, attaining 0.205 RMSE and 0.982 $\delta_{1}$ on NYU Depth v2, largely outperforming existing methods.
elements.sent-2411-125: In Fig.~\ref{figq1 , we present some qualitative comparison on the dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-126: First, one can observe that our model performs better in depth estimation at the boundaries of the objects, making it more coherent and smooth (e.g., the back of the chair and the long-distant objects).
elements.sent-2411-127: This is helped by our autoregressive progressive paradigm, which maintains a coherent and smooth depth estimation when using previous predictions for the next-step, more refined prediction.
elements.sent-2411-128: Second, our DAR is much more accurate when estimating the depths of small and thin objects or long-distant visually relatively small objects, like the poles under the chair.
elements.sent-2411-129: These observations further demonstrate the superiority of our DAR.
elements.sent-2411-130: To further demonstrate the superiority of our proposed DAR in outdoor scenarios, we report results on the KITTI dataset in Table~\ref{tab2 .}
elements.sent-2411-131: Compared to the SOTA self-supervised model Depth Anything, our DAR-small with a similar model size achieves better performance, suggesting the superiority and potential of our proposed DAR.
elements.sent-2411-132: Compared to the SOTA supervised model Ecodepth, our DAR-small has smaller model sizes and performs better.
elements.sent-2411-133: Notably, the scaled-up version DAR-Large establishes a new SOTA performance, which outperforms the current SOTA (Depth Anything) in all the metrics (in particular, by 5\% in RMSE).
elements.sent-2411-134: In Fig.~\ref{figq2 , we present some qualitative comparison on the KITTI dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-135: One can observe that DAR preserves fine-grained boundary details and generates more continuous depth values, further demonstrating the effectiveness of our new AR-based framework.
elements.sent-2411-136: Unlike the SOTA zero-shot transfer method (Depth Anything~\cite{yang2024depth ), which requires pretraining on a large amount of data (61M) for effective performance, we show that our model generalizes well when trained only on a single NYU Depth v2 dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-138: As one can see, DAR achieves decent results on unseen datasets, substantiating the generalization ability of DAR.

Abs Rel $\downarrow$ RMSE $\downarrow$ d1 $\uparrow$ Baseline + Transformer 420M 0.063 0.229 0.976 Baseline + MTBins + BI 363M 0.061 0.220 0.978 Baseline + DAR 440M 0.059 0.217 0.979 Baseline + DAR + Scale Up 2.0B 0.056 0.205 0.982 \end{tabular} } \label{taba}
elements.sent-2411-139: We conduct a comprehensive ablation study to examine the effectiveness of the two sub-AR objectives with their respective components and scale up.
elements.sent-2411-140: All the experiments presented in this section are conducted on NYU-Depth-V2.
elements.sent-2411-141: We choose the backbone of Depth Anything as the baseline model.
elements.sent-2411-142: The comparison results are reported in Table~\ref{taba .}
elements.sent-2411-143: Observe that each sub-AR objective and its components improve the baseline performance, supporting our hypothesis that the AR model is an effective monocular depth estimator.
elements.sent-2411-144: Furthermore, by scaling up the model size to 2.0B, DAR achieves the best performance in all the metrics, demonstrating the strong scalability of DAR.
elements.sent-2411-145: We also conduct ablation study on the bin number $N$, and show qualitative results of each step of DAR in the Supplemental Materials.
elements.sent-2411-146: We need to acknowledge the following limitations.
elements.sent-2411-147: First, we apply a multi-step progressive paradigm to predict depths, which is smoother and more continuous, but consequently, it may blur the boundaries and reduce sharpness.
elements.sent-2411-148: Second, since we use the autoregressive Transformer, the model parameter number of DAR is relatively high, especially when scaling the model size up.
elements.sent-2411-149: But, we believe that further improvements in the complexity-accuracy trade-off of DAR can be achieved through large model distillation or lightweight AR foundation model design techniques.
elements.sent-2411-150: In this paper, we presented a novel depth autoregressive (DAR) modeling approach to introduce scalability and generalization to an AR-based framework for monocular depth estimation (MDE).
elements.sent-2411-151: Our key idea is to transform the MDE task into two parallel autoregressive objectives, resolution and granularity, inspired by the insight of ordered properties of MDE in these two aspects.
elements.sent-2411-152: Further, we proposed the DAR Transformer and Multiway Tree Bins strategy to achieve these two autoregressive objectives, and connect them by the proposed Bin Injection module.
elements.sent-2411-153: We demonstrated the effectiveness of our approach on several benchmark datasets, and showed that it outperforms SOTA methods by a significant margin.
elements.sent-2411-154: Our proposed DAR also offers a promising way to integrate autoregressive depth estimation into existing autoregressive-based foundation models.. { \bibliographystyle{ieee_fullname.}}
elements.sent-2411-155: This paper shows that the autoregressive model is an effective and scalable monocular depth estimator.
elements.sent-2411-156: First, our depth autoregressive model (DAR) treats the depth map of different resolutions as a set of tokens, and conducts the low-to-high resolution autoregressive objective with a patch-wise causal mask.
elements.figure-2411-013: \includegraphics[width=0.85\textwidth]{figs/overview13.pdf} \caption{{\bf An overview of DAR.} We begin with encoding the input RGB images into image tokens as the context condition. At each step, DAR Transformer with the patch-wise causal mask performs autoregressive predictions, that is, it allows the input token map (upsampled from the previous resolution token map $r_{k-1}$) to interact with only the prefix tokens and global image feature tokens for the next-resolution token map modeling. The output latent tokens are then sent to the ConvGRU module, which injects the prompts of new refined bin candidates $\mathbf{c}_{k}$ (generated by MTBin from the previous prediction $\tilde{D}_{k-1}$) for further granularity guidance and generates the next-resolution token map $r_{k}$. The new depth map $\tilde{D}_{k}$ is generated by a linear combination of the next-granularity bin candidates $\mathbf{c}_{k}$ and softmax value $\mathbf{p}_{k}$ of the next-resolution token map, achieving concurrently a resolution and granularity autoregressive evolution. } \label{fig2} em
elements.figure-2411-014: \includegraphics[width=0.45\textwidth]{figs/444.pdf} \caption{Illustrating the patch-wise causal mask for ensuring that the current token map can interact only with tokens from itself and the prefix token maps.} \label{fig4} em
elements.sent-2411-157: DAR Transformer: Our DAR Transformer can progressively predict different resolution token maps conditioned on extracted RGB imaging tokens via the patch-wise causal mask.
